Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

Level 1: you have used 14 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Some RIGHT actions had 0 cells changed, meaning the green square hit a wall (probably the purple bar). The UP action only changed 1 cell (just the dot color?). Let me check the current positions to understand what happened.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('N', 'W', 'B'):
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"color={n['color']} center=({sum(rows)//len(rows)},{sum(cols)//len(cols)}) px={n['pixels']}")

lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nFull area:")
for i in range(26, 36):
    print(f"{i:2d}: {lines[i][8:55]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, so the green square is still at (31, 31). The UP changed 1 cell (probably the dot color from W to B or something), and the RIGHT actions mostly didn't move it because the purple bar is blocking.
Looking at the layout:
- The green square at (31, 31) is in the left panel area
- The purple bar (pppppp) is at cols ~39-44 (approximately)
- The right panel with the frame is to the right of the purple bar
The green square can't pass through the purple bar. So maybe the goal isn't to move the green squares between panels.
Let me reconsider the puzzle. Looking at the full board:
- Left panel: has a dark frame at (34, 13) and a green square at (34, 16)
- Middle: has a green square at (31, 31) and a purple vertical bar
- Right panel: has a dark frame at (28, 46)
Wait, the green square at (34, 16) is partially inside the left frame. Let me look more carefully:
Row 34: `gwwcwwwNcNwwwwwwgggggggggppppppwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwg`
This shows `cwwwNcN` - the frame border (c) and the green square (N) overlapping.
Actually, let me look at the left frame more carefully. The frame boundary is [[32, 11], [32, 15], [36, 15], [36, 11]], so it's rows 32-36, cols 11-15. The green square at (34, 16) is at cols 15-17, which overlaps with the frame at col 15.
Hmm, this is getting confusing. Let me think about this differently.
Maybe the goal is about the dot colors. The right frame has a white interior and a dark border. The left frame has a white interior and a dark border. Both green squares now have white dots. Maybe I need to make one green square have a black dot to match something?
Or maybe the goal is to have both green squares in their respective frames with matching dot colors.
Let me try a completely different approach. Let me click on the green square at (31, 31) again to toggle its dot back to black, and see if that changes anything.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the initial state more carefully. In the initial state:
- Left green square: white dot
- Right green square: black dot
- Left frame: white interior
- Right frame: white interior
Maybe the goal is to make the dot colors match the interior colors of the frames they're associated with? Both frames have white interiors, so both dots should be white. I already made the right green square have a white dot by clicking on it.
But the level isn't done. Maybe I need to also place the green squares INTO the frames?
Let me try clicking on the left green square (at 34, 16) to select it, then move it into the left frame.
Wait, but the left green square is already partially in the left frame. Let me look at the ASCII again:
Row 34: `gwwcwwwNcNwwwwwwgggggggggppppppwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwg`
The frame is cols 11-15. The green square at (34, 16) would be cols 15-17. So it's partially overlapping.
Let me try to move the left green square fully into the left frame. From (34, 16), I need to go LEFT to (34, 13).
But first I need to select it by clicking.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me click on the left green square to select it
# It's at center (34, 16)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 34, 'col': 16}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
